slides.description: The slide is titled "AI Screenplays" in large blue text at the top. The main image depicts a detailed scene from the animated TV show "South Park," set within a well-appointed office overlooking the Hollywood sign at sunset. A character resembling a studio executive, dressed in a black jacket and red tie, stands next to the cardboard robot "A.W.E.S.O.M.-O" (Awesom-O), which is prominently featured. The office includes a large wooden desk adorned with golden award statues, including Oscar-like and Emmy-like trophies, and a red collectible figure of Cartman. On the wall to the right, a framed picture shows "AWESOM-O" with a red stamp reading "LAME." Documents resembling screenplays are visible on the desk, alongside a rotary phone and a framed family photo. This introductory slide visually establishes the topic of AI in screenwriting using a recognizable, satirical pop culture reference to Awesom-O, a character known for generating uninspired movie ideas.

This slide is titled "Generating Long Documents" in large blue text at the top. It features four bullet points outlining the core concept and objective. The first bullet states that "AI can be used to generate long, coherent text documents," followed by the second bullet listing examples such as "Books, reports, screenplays." The third bullet point specifies the lecture's focus: "Today we will build an agentic flow to generate screenplays for movies." The final bullet indicates the broader applicability of the method: "This flow architecture can be generalized to other long text documents." This slide transitions from the satirical introduction of AI screenplays on the previous slide to a more formal overview of the capabilities of AI in generating extended text and outlines the practical goal of the lecture.

This slide is titled "Challenge: 'One-Shot' Long-Form" in large blue text, immediately following the introduction of AI's ability to generate long documents. It highlights the primary difficulty an AI faces in generating a long, coherent document in a single attempt. The slide explains this challenge through the concept of "local vs global" coherence, noting that AI excels at writing small parts but struggles to maintain overall consistency across an entire document. Specific issues like the AI becoming "lost in the middle" and experiencing "context drift" are mentioned, where the AI can hallucinate or lose focus deep into the text. This slide effectively pivots from the potential of AI in long text generation to the practical limitations that necessitate a more sophisticated approach, implicitly setting up the need for the "agentic flow" mentioned on Slide 2.

The slide is titled "Movie Screenplays" in large blue text. It presents two main bullet points detailing the complexity of screenplays. The first bullet explains that "Screenplays have many different elements the AI needs to capture," listing "Story shape," "Goals of 'sequences' in the movie," and "Scene action and dialogue" as examples. The second main bullet, "Hierarchical structure of a screenplay," breaks down a screenplay's organization into progressively detailed levels: "Premise – basic elements of the movie," "Story arc – high level plot structure," "Sequences – more detailed plot," and "Scenes – even more detailed plot." This slide elaborates on the challenge of generating long-form content, specifically screenplays, by illustrating their inherent structural complexity, which was implied as a difficulty for AI in the previous slide's discussion of 'one-shot' generation and context drift.

The slide is titled "Screenplay Flow" in large blue text at the top. It features a flowchart diagram that visually outlines an agentic process for generating screenplays, building upon the hierarchical structure introduced in the previous slide. The flow begins with a blue rectangular node labeled "Premise" with "premise.json" underneath, leading via an arrow to a purple rectangular node labeled "Arc" with "arc.json." From the "Arc" node, two arrows diverge: one points to an orange rectangular node labeled "Sequences" with "sequence.json," and the other points downwards to a green rectangular node labeled "Scenes" with "scenes.json." Each node includes a small icon resembling a brown box with eyes, possibly indicating an agent or data processing step. This diagram concretely illustrates the 'agentic flow' architecture mentioned earlier, demonstrating how AI can generate long documents by breaking down the complex hierarchical structure of a screenplay into sequential, manageable steps to address 'one-shot' generation challenges.

The slide is titled "Premise Agent" in large blue text and details the function of the first agent in the screenplay generation flow. It describes a chat agent that discusses the core concept of a movie with the user, covering elements like title, logline, characters, genre, and central conflict. The agent can provide plot ideas if the user is stuck, and the conversational process is noted to take a few minutes to solidify all major elements. This agent's output is a "premise.json" file, directly corresponding to the initial 'Premise' node in the flowchart from Slide 5. A small image of the A.W.E.S.O.M.-O robot is present in the top right corner, continuing the visual motif introduced in Slide 1.

The slide is titled "Premise.json" in large blue text, directly displaying the output of the 'Premise Agent' discussed in the previous slide. The main content is a code block showing a JSON file named 'premise.json', which encapsulates the core concept of a movie. Key fields visible include a 'project_id', 'title' ("Shadow of the Dark World"), 'writers' (listed as "TBD"), and 'format_notes' indicating "Awesome-O generated this from a CLI chat." The JSON also contains a detailed 'logline' describing a quest involving characters reminiscent of 'The Legend of Zelda', along with 'genre' (fantasy, action, adventure) and 'themes' (hero's journey, destiny, etc.). This slide provides a tangible example of the structured data generated during the initial premise-setting step of the agentic screenplay flow, illustrating how the foundational elements of a movie are captured before further development.

The slide is titled "Premise.json" in large blue text, continuing the display of the JSON output from the 'Premise Agent' discussed in earlier slides. The main content is a code block showcasing further details of the 'premise.json' file, which outlines the foundational elements of a movie concept. This section of the JSON explicitly details the "central_conflict" for the movie, describing Link's quest to stop Aghanim and Ganon from destroying Hyrule. It also elaborates on the movie's "setting," including the "primary_time" as a high fantasy era and listing specific "primary_places" like Hyrule Castle and Kakariko Village. Furthermore, the JSON presents details for the "protagonists" array, clearly defining 'Link' with his role as an "unassuming hero" and his primary 'want.' This slide serves to further illustrate the granular detail captured by the initial agent in the screenplay generation process.

The slide is titled "Premise.json" in large blue text, continuing the display of the foundational movie concept from the 'Premise Agent.' It showcases further details within the JSON code block, specifically focusing on the main characters and the overarching stakes of the story. The JSON defines the 'protagonists' array, clearly listing Link with his role as an "unassuming hero" and his primary 'want' to save Hyrule. It then details the 'key_antagonist' as Aghanim, describing his role as Ganon's pawn and his goal to release Ganon. A 'key_ally,' Sahasrahla, is also defined with his role as a guide and his objective to help Link acquire the Master Sword. Finally, the 'stakes' for the movie are explicitly stated as "The total conquest of the Light World by the dark lord Ganon." This slide completes the comprehensive overview of the 'premise.json' file, illustrating how all critical high-level story elements are established before moving to subsequent stages of the screenplay generation flow.

The slide is titled "Arc Agent" in large blue text at the top, featuring the A.W.E.S.O.M.-O robot icon in the top right corner. This slide describes the next agent in the screenplay generation process, following the Premise Agent. It details a chat agent that discusses the movie's story arc, which consists of multiple acts. The agent explicitly takes the `premise.json` file as input for context, and users guide it by telling it what happens in each act, prompting it to draft them one at a time. The agent then creates an `arc.json` file. This slide elaborates on the 'Arc' stage from the 'Screenplay Flow' diagram (Slide 5), building directly upon the foundational `premise.json` content established in the preceding slides.

The slide is titled "Arc.json" in large blue text, directly showcasing the output of the 'Arc Agent' introduced in the previous slide. The main content is a code block displaying a JSON file, specifically detailing 'Act 3' of the movie's story arc. The JSON includes a 'label' for this act ("Battle Aghanim and the Dark World"), an 'approx_pages_note' indicating it will be 20-30 pages of a 120-page screenplay, and a 'dramatic_question' outlining the core conflict. A comprehensive 'summary' details the plot points of Act 3, including Link's quest to defeat Aghanim and prevent Ganon's rise. It also features a 'turning_point_end' description and a list of 'must_hit_beats' for the act. This slide provides a tangible example of how the high-level story premise is developed into a structured, act-by-act narrative with detailed plot points, fulfilling the 'Arc' stage of the agentic screenplay generation flow.

The slide is titled "Sequence Agent" in large blue text at the top, continuing the visual motif with the A.W.E.S.O.M.-O robot icon in the top right corner. This slide describes the next agent in the screenplay generation flow, distinguishing it as an "automated agent (no chat)" responsible for writing the movie sequences for each act. It specifies that the agent's inputs include both the 'Premise.json' and 'Arc.json' files for global context, along with all previously generated sequences for local context. The primary elements it focuses on for each sequence are its narrative goal, key locations, and the 'exit beat' for transition. This agent ultimately creates a 'sequence.json' file, directly corresponding to the 'Sequences' node in the 'Screenplay Flow' diagram (Slide 5) and building upon the outputs of the previous Premise and Arc agents.

The slide is titled "Sequence.json" in large blue text, directly displaying the output of the 'Sequence Agent' introduced in the previous slide. The main content is a code block showcasing a JSON file, specifically detailing the first sequence of the movie. This 'sequence.json' includes a 'project_id' and 'sequence_count', and defines the initial sequence as 'SEQ1' with the title "Zelda's Urgent Plea." It specifies this sequence belongs to 'act' 1 and outlines its 'goal' to establish Link's call to adventure, his motivation, and to equip him for his journey. The JSON also details the 'emotional_tone' as "Urgent, sorrowful, determined," lists 'key_locations' like "Link's Home" and "Hyrule Castle (Outer Walls)," and provides an 'exit_beat' describing Link's resolve to infiltrate Hyrule Castle. This slide tangibly illustrates how the high-level story arc is broken down into detailed, actionable sequences, fulfilling the 'Sequences' stage of the agentic screenplay generation flow.

The slide is titled "Scene Agent" in large blue text, continuing the series describing the components of the agentic screenplay generation flow, with the A.W.E.S.O.M.-O robot icon in the top right. This slide describes the final agent, an "automated agent (no chat)" responsible for writing the individual scenes within each sequence. It specifies that this agent takes multiple inputs for context: `Premise.json`, `Arc.json`, and `Sequence.json` for a global understanding of the movie, plus all previously generated scenes within the current and prior sequences for local context. The primary elements the agent focuses on are the "Slug line" (location of the scene) and "Blocks" (actions and dialogues). Ultimately, this agent creates a `scenes.json` file, representing the most granular level of the screenplay, and directly corresponds to the 'Scenes' node in the overall 'Screenplay Flow' diagram (Slide 5), building upon all prior stages.

The slide is titled "Scenes.json" in large blue text, directly displaying the output of the 'Scene Agent' introduced previously. The main content is a code block showcasing the initial portion of a JSON file, specifically detailing the first scene of the movie. This 'scenes.json' defines a scene identified as 'S0001', with the slugline 'INT. LINK'S HOME - NIGHT' and belonging to 'SEQ1'. The JSON structure includes an array of 'blocks', which detail the scene's actions and dialogue. It describes Link waking up to a desperate, telepathic whisper from Zelda, who is trapped in Hyrule Castle by Aghanim. This slide provides a tangible example of the granular level of a generated screenplay, fulfilling the 'Scenes' stage of the agentic flow by illustrating how the high-level narrative is translated into specific actions and dialogue.

The slide is titled "Enhancement Agents" in large blue text, introducing a new type of agent within the screenplay generation process. It features a main bullet point explaining that the agentic flow can be improved by using agents to enhance intermediate outputs, followed by sub-bullets listing examples such as writing better sequences or scenes, cleaning up the premise, and making the arc more creative. In the top right, a gold-colored robot icon labeled "ENHANCEMENT AGENT" is displayed, continuing the visual motif. At the bottom, the "Screenplay Flow" diagram from Slide 5 is reproduced, showing the connections between "Premise," "Arc," "Sequences," and "Scenes." Crucially, small robot icons, representing these enhancement agents, are now positioned on the arrows between these nodes, visually indicating their role in refining the outputs (e.g., premise.json, arc.json) before they are passed to subsequent stages. This slide proposes a mechanism to elevate the quality and consistency of the screenplay by refining the content generated by the primary agents discussed in previous slides.

The slide is titled "Generalizing to Other Documents" in large blue text. A single bullet point states that "By modifying the agent prompts, this hierarchical flow can be used for other long text documents." Below this, a table is displayed with columns titled "Document," "Premise," "Arc," "Sequence," "Scene," and "Artist Rendering." The table illustrates how the hierarchical structure previously applied to screenplays can be adapted for five different document types: Book (with 'Premise', 'Table of contents', 'Chapter', 'Sub-chapter'), Podcast (with 'Concept', 'Flow, format', 'Segment', 'Beat'), Song (with 'Premise', 'Structure (verse, hooks, coda)', 'Verse', 'Couplet'), Lecture (with 'Learning objective', 'Outline', 'Module', 'Slide'), and Business plan (with 'Value prop', 'Outline', 'Section', 'Sub-section'). Each row also features a small image in the 'Artist Rendering' column, often depicting the A.W.E.S.O.M.-O robot in a relevant context for that document type. This slide directly builds upon the 'agentic flow' and hierarchical breakdown introduced for screenplays, demonstrating the broader applicability of the method, as was initially hinted at in Slide 2.

The slide is titled "Coding Session" in large blue text, indicating a practical, hands-on segment of the lecture. It presents three bullet points outlining tasks or objectives for the session: "Write a short screenplay using Awesome-O," "Add enhancement agents to the agentic flow," and "Modify Awesome-O for different document types." Below these points, the "Screenplay Flow" diagram from Slide 5 is reproduced, illustrating the connections between "Premise," "Arc," "Sequences," and "Scenes." Each node features a brown box-with-eyes icon, and significantly, small robot icons representing "Enhancement Agents" (introduced in Slide 16) are now depicted on the arrows connecting these nodes. This slide marks a transition from theoretical explanation to practical application, challenging the audience to implement the hierarchical agentic flow for screenplay generation and its generalizations, leveraging the concepts of core agents and enhancement agents discussed in preceding slides.
